content.text:
Analyze @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md review the tone "cancel rules" in the contex of the whole specification to find any gaps, contradictions...

Don't change any content. Incomplete example is acceptable.

Discuss with me to improve the specification.

content.thinking: I need to look at the spec file and understand what the cancel rules are for tone in context.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Diacritics
8: 
9: Diacritics let a character has many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
10: Tones have seperated section.
11: 
12: #### Empty
13: 
14: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
15: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
16:   characters.
17: 
18: #### Circumflex
19: 
20: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
21: 
22: #### Breve
23: 
24: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
25: 
26: #### Horn
27: 
28: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
29: 
30: #### Stroke
31: 
32: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
33: 
34: #### Apply rules
35: 
36: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
37: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
38:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
39:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
40:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
41: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
42:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
43:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
44:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
45: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
46: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
47: 
48: #### Override rules
49: 
50: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
51:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
52:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
53: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
54: - Keep tone untouched.
55: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
56: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
57: 
58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the new word.
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
72: 
73: #### Auto-fill rules
74: 
75: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
76:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
77: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
78:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
79:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
80:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
81: - The new input character is appended literally.
82: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
83: 
84: ### Tones
85: 
86: Tones let a word has many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
87: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
88: These vowel could take any tone.
89: 
90: #### Level (ngang)
91: 
92: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
93:   corresponding variants.
94: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
95: 
96: #### Rising (sắc)
97: 
98: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
99: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
100:   -> `Ớ`.
101: 
102: #### Falling (huyền)
103: 
104: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
105: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
106:   -> `Ờ`.
107: 
108: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
109: 
110: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
112:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
113: 
114: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
115: 
116: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
118:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
119: 
120: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
121: 
122: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
124:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
125: 
126: #### Apply rules
127: 
128: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
129:   character literally.
130: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
131:   their lower cases.
132: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
133: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
134: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowels and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
135:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
136: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
137: 
138: #### Cancel rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowels and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match
146:   with the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
147: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
148:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
149: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
150: 
151: #### Reset rules
152: 
153: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
154:   character literally.
155: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
156: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
157: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowels and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
158:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
159: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowels and all of them don't have
160:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
161: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
162:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
163: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
164: 
165: #### Scan pseudo-word
166: 
167: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
168:   the tone in the correct position.
169: - The existing characters must not be empty.
170: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
171: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
172:   character to scan, then stop.
173: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
174:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
175: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
176:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
177:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
178: 
179: #### Tone position rules
180: 
181: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
182: 
183: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
184:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
185: - Exceptions:
186:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
187: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
188: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
189:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
190:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
191:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
192:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
193:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
194:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
195:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
196:     consonants, put on second vowel.
197:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
198: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
199: - Could not put tone for other cases.

content.thinking:
I'm examining the cancel rules for tones in the spec, which define when a tone marker gets reset to level tone and replaced with a literal character—specifically when the buffer already has content and a new S/F/R/X/J input is detected, triggering a scan of the pseudo-word to check if any vowels match the incoming tone. Let me trace through where this logic fits in the overall flow.

I'm noticing several gaps in how the cancel rules interact with the apply rules. Both sets trigger on the same input characters, but there's no explicit precedence defined—it's unclear which one should be checked first or how the system decides between them. The cancel rules also seem to duplicate the first three conditions from the apply rules (checking buffer state, input type, and pseudo-word scanning), which suggests these might need to be unified or the dispatch mechanism made explicit. There's also ambiguity around what happens when the triggered tone doesn't match any existing tone on the vowels—the apply rules require all vowels to be toneless, and the cancel rules only trigger on a match, so a case like changing from rising to falling tone appears to fall through both rule sets entirely. And the cancel rule description cuts off mid-sentence, so I'm not seeing the complete picture of what happens after the tone switch occurs. The Reset rules don't explicitly say what happens to the trigger character `z` itself — whether it gets appended or consumed — which creates an inconsistency with how Cancel handles its trigger. I'm also noticing gaps around edge cases: what happens when Cancel rules apply to consonant-only pseudo-words, or when there are multiple vowels with different tones in the same word. The Cancel section itself feels fragmentary, only documenting the matching case without addressing what happens when tones don't match or vowels are absent. There's also a question about whether the scan-pseudo-word logic properly handles cases where a matching tone might exist outside the user's intended boundaries.

Looking at the interaction between Cancel and Reset, there's an asymmetry I need to understand: Cancel (matching tone key) resets the tone and enters literal mode, while Reset (Z) resets any tone without entering literal mode. The reasoning seems to be that Cancel implies the user double-typed a tone key and wants the literal letter, whereas Reset is intentional. But there's a real gap when a user types a consonant that doesn't match the current tone — the spec doesn't say whether to append it literally, replace the tone, or leave it undefined. Most Vietnamese Telex IMEs would replace the tone, but the spec is silent. I'm also noticing that the first three bullets of the Cancel rules are exact copies of the Apply rules, which suggests these might be better combined into a single dispatch logic that checks for matching tones first, then falls back to apply rules, then handles the undefined case.

There's also ambiguity around whether Cancel preserves existing diacritics — Reset explicitly says to keep case and diacritics, but Cancel doesn't mention it. And when a user types `Áa` (existing rising-tone A followed by a), the diacritic apply rules would create `Ấ` since the tone is preserved separately from the diacritic. But there's a deeper issue: once Cancel removes a tone, the user has lost it, and backspace would only remove the new literal character, not restore the tone. The spec doesn't clarify whether undoing a cancel is even possible.

Let me trace through the Apply rules more carefully — they only trigger if all vowels are at level tone, and if Apply can't place the tone, it falls back to Cancel. But if Cancel doesn't match either, the system seems to do nothing, which feels like a gap in the logic.

content.text:
I read the spec. Here's my analysis of the tone **Cancel rules** (lines 138–149) in the context of the whole document.

## Gaps

### 1. No dispatch between Apply / Cancel / Reset
All three sections share the same first three bullets ("buffer not empty", "trigger on S/F/R/X/J or Z", "scan pseudo-word"). The spec never says which rule wins when they could overlap, or in what order they're evaluated. A single decision table or dispatch description is missing.

### 2. Tone-replace case is undefined
The most important gap. Consider `Á` (rising) + user types `f`:
- Apply rules require *all vowels level* → don't trigger.
- Cancel rules require *trigger matches existing tone* → don't trigger (`f` ≠ rising).
- Reset rules trigger only on `Z`/`z`.

So `Áf` falls through every rule. The spec doesn't say whether the result is `Àf` (replace tone, the common Telex behavior), `Áf` literal, or something else.

### 3. Cancel only describes the "match" branch
Bullet 4 (line 145–146) only covers the case where the triggered tone matches an existing tone. It doesn't say what happens for:
- pseudo-word with no vowels,
- pseudo-word with vowels but no tone,
- pseudo-word with a *different* tone than the trigger.

These are presumably handled by Apply rules, but the spec doesn't connect them.

### 4. Cancel doesn't mention diacritic preservation
Reset rules explicitly say "keep case and diacritic" (line 158). Cancel rules say only "Reset that tone to level" (line 146). It's implied but not stated that diacritic and case are preserved on the existing vowel. Compare with diacritic Cancel rules (line 62) which explicitly state "keeps its case, tone".

### 5. Asymmetry between Reset and Cancel — not explained
- **Cancel** match: reset tone + append trigger literally + enter literal mode.
- **Reset** success: reset tone, but the spec doesn't say whether `z` is consumed or appended, and doesn't enter literal mode.
- **Reset** fallback (no tone to reset): append literally + literal mode.

The fate of `z` in the successful Reset case is undefined. Cancel explicitly appends; Reset is silent.

### 6. "Match" semantics not defined
"Match with the triggered tone" (line 145) — does `s` only match rising on the *vowel where tone position rules would have placed it*, or any vowel in the pseudo-word that happens to carry rising? Since only one tone exists per word in practice, this likely doesn't matter, but the spec should pin it down.

### 7. Backspace recovery not specified
After a successful cancel (`Ás` → `As` literal), backspacing the `s` leaves `A`. The tone `Á` is lost. The spec mentions backspace switches modes (line 149) but says nothing about restoring the canceled tone. Probably intentional, but worth stating.

### 8. Redundancy hiding a real structural choice
Lines 140–144 are an exact copy of lines 128–132. This duplication signals that Apply / Cancel / Reset should probably be presented as one dispatcher with branches, rather than three parallel sections each pretending to be self-contained.

## Contradictions

I didn't find a hard contradiction, but the closest concern:

- Diacritic Cancel rules (line 62) say "keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed." Tone Cancel rules omit the symmetric statement about diacritic. The patterns should mirror each other for clarity.

## Suggested discussion points

1. Decide and document the **tone-replace** semantics (gap 2). This is the highest-impact gap.
2. Restructure Apply / Cancel / Reset as one tone-trigger dispatcher with explicit branches, eliminating duplicated bullets.
3. State preservation of case and diacritic in Cancel explicitly.
4. Clarify whether `Z` is consumed or appended in the successful Reset branch.
5. State that canceled tones are not restored on backspace (or specify if they should be).

Which of these would you like to address first?
